summary: Jeff Atwood observes that software development discussions tend toward extreme polarization, with people either loving or hating technologies rather than holding moderate views. He compares this to religious arguments and partisan political theater like Crossfire. While acknowledging that strongly polarized opinions can distort discourse (as seen in Amazon reviews), he argues that controversy has value: products that inspire strong reactions tend to be more interesting and successful than mediocre ones that elicit indifference. The key is maintaining perspective while creating things bold enough to inspire passion.
key_points: Software development debates mirror religious arguments, with people taking extreme partisan positions on everything from languages to tabs vs spaces
Online reviews suffer from selection bias - only those with extreme positive or negative experiences are motivated to write them
Controversy is actually desirable: products that inspire both love and hate tend to be more successful than mediocre ones
The worst reaction to your work is indifference ('Meh'), not hatred
While the love/hate dichotomy has value, it's important not to frame all discussions in purely partisan terms
money_quotes: Software development is basically a religion.
Heavily partisan discussion – lovers on one side, haters on another – isn't conducive to civilized discourse.
It's not debate at all; it's pure theater. Entertainment. Professional wrestling, if you will.
What about the vast, silent audience who liked it despite its flaws?
If nobody cares enough about what you're doing to tell you that they loved it or that they hated it, it's probably not interesting enough to be good, either.
The last reaction you want to anything you've done is a resounding 'Meh.'
Go out of your way to create something that will inspire people to love it. Unfortunately, that means a few people will probably hate it, too.
key_insight: Creating something genuinely interesting means accepting that strong negative reactions are the inevitable flip side of passionate fans, and both are preferable to indifference.
